nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still waters including ponds, ditches, pools, and heavily vegetated rivers and lakes
nativeRange.biotope: Shallow, heavily vegetated standing or slow-moving freshwater with soft, often acidic conditions

decorPreferences: Dense planting — tall stem plants and floating plants for surface cover
Leaf litter or driftwood for tannins and shelter
Dark substrate to reduce stress and bring out colour

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality flake and micro-pellet foods. Benefits from regular variety including frozen or live Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm, and small insects such as fruit flies. Tends to feed at or near the surface.

goodWith: Small peaceful tetras (e.g. ember tetras, green neon tetras)
Small loaches (e.g. kuhli loach, dwarf chain loach)
Adult dwarf shrimp (cherry shrimp, Amano shrimp) with caution
avoidWith: Aggressive or fin-nipping fish (e.g. tiger barbs)
Large boisterous fish that will outcompete or intimidate
Dwarf Gourami — may compete for territory and territory overlap can cause stress

summary: A gentle labyrinth fish from India and Bangladesh (Trichogaster chuna), widely considered one of the more beginner-friendly gouramis available. Generally regarded as less aggressive and less prone to disease than the Dwarf Gourami.
careNotes:
The Honey Gourami is a labyrinth fish native to slow-moving and still waters of India and Bangladesh. The older scientific name Colisa chuna is still encountered in the trade and older literature, but the species is currently classified as Trichogaster chuna. Like all labyrinth fish, it possesses a labyrinth organ — a specialized accessory breathing structure that allows it to take in oxygen directly from the air — and a tight-fitting lid is advisable both to prevent escape and to keep a layer of warm, humid air above the water surface.

Males tend to be more colourful, displaying rich honey-orange to red-orange colouration, and develop a dark blue-black throat patch when breeding or displaying. Females are generally smaller and more subdued in colour. Several selectively bred colour strains are sold in the hobby — notably the 'Sunset Honey Gourami' (vivid orange) and 'Red Honey Gourami' (deeper red-orange); wild-caught specimens tend to be more drab.

This species is generally regarded as notably healthier than the Dwarf Gourami in the hobby trade, owing to less intensive inbreeding in captive lines. It is commonly considered less prone to the viral illness known as Dwarf Gourami Iridovirus that frequently affects Dwarf Gouramis.

Males are mildly territorial toward each other but far less so than Dwarf Gourami. In tanks of 75 litres or more, a pair or small group (one male with one to two females) generally coexists without significant conflict. A group of four to six specimens is reported to produce more natural behaviour and better colouration displays. Dense planting reduces aggression by breaking sightlines. Floating plants are particularly valued as they provide cover near the surface where the fish naturally patrol.

The species tends to be shy and slow-moving; fast or boisterous tankmates may intimidate it and suppress feeding. It is generally best kept with similarly-paced, peaceful community fish.
breedingNotes: The Honey Gourami is a bubble-nest builder. Males construct floating nests among surface plants or beneath broad leaves, and conditioning both fish with live or frozen foods tends to trigger spawning behaviour. After spawning, the male guards the nest actively and may become aggressive toward the female; removing the female at this point is generally recommended to reduce stress on her. Eggs typically hatch within 24 to 36 hours depending on temperature. The fry are initially very small and have been reported to require infusoria (microscopic organisms that grow naturally in aged, plant-rich water) or commercially prepared fry powder before they are large enough to accept baby brine shrimp nauplii at around two weeks of age. Breeding is considered moderate in difficulty, with the bubble-nest requirement and the small size of the fry being the primary challenges.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (45–55 mm body), water parameters, native range, grouping minimum (4-6 recommended), and habitat description. pH upper end 7.5 consistent with this source — used 7.5 rather than AqCoop's 8.0. Minimum tank base 60×30 cm (~54L) referenced; rounded up to 75L given grouping and activity space recommendations.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Confirmed lifespan 4–8 years, gH 4–15 dGH, pH 6.0–7.5, temperature 71–82°F (21.7–27.8°C), beginner care level. FishBase reports 13.7 cm TL which likely includes elongated filamentous rays; hobby consensus of 4–5.5 cm body length used.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmed: family Osphronemidae, order Anabantiformes. Native range India and Bangladesh. Temperature 22–28°C. Max TL 13.7 cm noted but not used for adultSizeCm — TL measurement likely includes fin filaments; standard body length consistent with hobby sources (~4–5.5 cm).

fish.breedingNotes: The male builds a floating bubble nest and guards it after spawning. The female should be removed once eggs have been laid to avoid stress and aggression. Fry are very small at hatching and require infusoria or powdered fry food before they can accept larger foods.
